content: Entire CLI will link this conversation to your next commit.

content.text:
I don't like ls and ll turning into abbreviations along with a few others that were originally fish functions. Look at it a few commits back. It is quite noisy to have ls expand into the long command.

Some of the abbreviations make sense. Can we keep the ones that were functions originally in fish as functions going forward (both in fish and zsh)?

thoughts.subject: Reverting Functions
thoughts.description: I'm currently reviewing past commits to reinstate `ls` and `ll` as functions in both `fish` and `zsh`, aiming to reduce the verbosity from long command expansions. This should streamline the user experience.

thoughts.subject: Reverting Functions
thoughts.description: I'm currently reviewing past commits to reinstate `ls` and `ll` as functions in both `fish` and `zsh`, aiming to reduce the verbosity from long command expansions. This should streamline the user experience.

toolCalls.name: update_topic

args.summary: I am starting an investigation into the recent changes that converted fish functions into abbreviations. The user prefers these to remain as functions to avoid noisy expansions in the terminal. I will examine the git history to see the original implementations and then plan the revert for both fish and zsh.
args.strategic_intent: Investigating the recent changes to aliases and abbreviations to identify which functions were converted.
args.title: Researching Alias/Function History

toolCalls.resultDisplay:
## 📂 Topic: **Researching Alias/Function History**

**Summary:**
I am starting an investigation into the recent changes that converted fish functions into abbreviations. The user prefers these to remain as functions to avoid noisy expansions in the terminal. I will examine the git history to see the original implementations and then plan the revert for both fish and zsh.

> [!STRATEGY]
> **Intent:** Investigating the recent changes to aliases and abbreviations to identify which functions were converted.

response.output:
aliases:
  # General
  l: ll -a
  ll: ls -l
  ls: eza -gF --group-directories-first --git

  # Directory Navigation
  "..": cd ..
  "...": cd ../..
  "....": cd ../../..
  ".....": cd ../../../..
  "......": cd ../../../../..

  # Git
  g: git
  gf: git fetch
  gfa: git fetch --all
  gfo: git fetch origin
  gs: git status
  gs.: git status .
  gc: git commit -m
  gm: git merge FETCH_HEAD --ff-only
  gr: git rb FETCH_HEAD
  ga: git add
  ga.: git add .
  gp: git push
  gd: git diff
  gd.: git diff .
  gco: git checkout

  # GitHub PRs
  gpc: gh pr create -f
  gps: gh pr status
  gpm: gh pr merge

  # Other
  bs: brew services
  cw: cd ~/work

response.output:
emptytrash() {
    if [ "$(uname)" = "Darwin" ]; then
        sudo rm -rfv /Volumes/*/.Trashes
        sudo rm -rfv ~/.Trash
        sudo rm -rfv /private/var/log/asl/*.asl
        sqlite3 ~/Library/Preferences/com.apple.LaunchServices.QuarantineEventsV* 'delete from LSQuarantineEvent'
    else
        # Linux / Freedesktop
        if [ -d "$HOME/.local/share/Trash" ]; then
            rm -rfv "$HOME/.local/share/Trash"/*
            echo "=> Linux Trash emptied."
        else
            echo "=> No standard Trash directory found."
        fi
    fi
}

thoughts.subject: Refining Alias Management
thoughts.description: I'm re-evaluating the alias strategy. The initial plan to move all "noisy" abbreviations to `functions` in `aliases.yaml` needs adjustment. The user's clarification about preserving original fish functions as functions in both shell environments requires a nuanced approach.

thoughts.subject: Structuring Alias Definitions
thoughts.description: I've decided to restructure `aliases.yaml`. All explicit command aliases will reside under the `aliases` key, while those intended to be functions in both zsh and fish will be moved to a new `functions` key, ensuring broader compatibility and desired expansion behavior across shells.

thoughts.subject: Identifying Alias Conflicts
thoughts.description: I'm reviewing abbreviations for potential "noise," noting that `ls`'s extensive expansion is a prime candidate. I've also uncovered a conflict with `cw`, which exists as both a fish function and an alias, necessitating a consolidation strategy.

thoughts.subject: Consolidating Function Definitions
thoughts.description: I'm realizing that complex, multi-line functions shouldn't live in `aliases.yaml`. Only simple one-liners like `ls` and `ll` are suitable for consolidation there. I'll move those into the `functions` section of `aliases.yaml` and retain the more intricate ones as separate files.

toolCalls.resultDisplay:
## 📂 Topic: **Refactoring Alias Configuration**

**Summary:**
I have identified the noisy abbreviations (ls, ll, l, and navigation dots) that were previously fish functions. I will now restructure .chezmoidata/aliases.yaml to separate these into a 'functions' section. This will allow me to define them as proper functions (or non-expanding aliases) in both fish and zsh, fulfilling the user's request for a less noisy terminal experience.

> [!STRATEGY]
> **Intent:** Refactoring aliases and functions in .chezmoidata/aliases.yaml to distinguish between abbreviations and non-expanding functions.

aliases:
  # General
  l: ll -a
  ll: ls -l
  ls: eza -gF --group-directories-first --git

  # Directory Navigation
  "..": cd ..
  "...": cd ../..
  "....": cd ../../..
  ".....": cd ../../../..
  "......": cd ../../../../..

  # Git
  g: git
  gf: git fetch
  gfa: git fetch --all
  gfo: git fetch origin
  gs: git status
  gs.: git status .
  gc: git commit -m
  gm: git merge FETCH_HEAD --ff-only
  gr: git rb FETCH_HEAD
  ga: git add

aliases:
  # Git
  g: git
  gf: git fetch
  gfa: git fetch --all
  gfo: git fetch origin
  gs: git status
  gs.: git status .
  gc: git commit -m
  gm: git merge FETCH_HEAD --ff-only
  gr: git rb FETCH_HEAD
  ga: git add
  ga.: git add .
  gp: git push
  gd: git diff
  gd.: git diff .
  gco: git checkout

  # GitHub PRs
  gpc: gh pr create -f
  gps: gh pr status
  gpm: gh pr merge

  # Other
  bs: brew services

functions:
  # General
  l: ll -a
  ll: ls -l
  ls: eza -gF --group-directories-first --git

  # Directory Navigation
  "..": cd ..
  "...": cd ../..
  "....": cd ../../..
  ".....": cd ../../../..
  "......": cd ../../../../..

  # Other
  cw: cd ~/work

content:
I've refactored your alias and function configuration to address the noise caused by abbreviation expansion.

### Changes Made:

1.  **Restructured `.chezmoidata/aliases.yaml`**:
    *   Split the configuration into `aliases` (which continue to be treated as abbreviations for quick expansion) and a new `functions` section.
    *   Moved `ls`, `ll`, `l`, `cw`, and all navigation dots (`..` through `......`) to the `functions` section. These will now behave as proper shell functions and will **not expand** in your terminal when typed.

2.  **Updated Shell Templates**:
    *   **Fish**: Updated `dot_config/fish/private_conf.d/aliases.fish.tmpl` to iterate over the new `functions` data and define them using the `function` keyword.
    *   **Zsh**: Updated `dot_zshrc.tmpl` to define these as standard Zsh functions. They are also excluded from `zsh-abbr`, so they won't expand there either.

3.  **Cleaned Up Redundancy**:
    *   Removed `dot_config/fish/private_functions/private_cw.fish` since it is now managed centrally in `aliases.yaml`.

This setup gives you the best of both worlds: concise abbreviations for things like `gs` (git status) where expansion is helpful, and quiet functions for common commands like `ls` and `cd` shortcuts where expansion is distracting.

[Active Topic: Finalizing Refactor]

toolCalls.name: activate_skill

response.output:
<activated_skill name="conventional-commits">
  <instructions>
    # Conventional Commits

Write every commit message according to the [Conventional Commits v1.0.0](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/en/v1.0.0/) specification.

## Strict Rule: No Agent Attribution

**CRITICAL:** You must NEVER add `Co-Authored-By`, `Signed-off-by`, or any other trailer that attributes or identifies an AI agent as a contributor. The commit message MUST be indistinguishable from one written by a human developer. Do not include any mentions of AI, bots, or agent involvement in the description, body, or footers. This rule is absolute and must be enforced strictly for every commit.

## Commit message format

```
<type>[optional scope]: <description>

[optional body]

[optional footer(s)]
```

## Rules

### Type (required)

The first element is the **type** — a noun that categorizes the change:

| Type       | When to use                                          | SemVer effect |
|------------|------------------------------------------------------|---------------|
| `feat`     | A new feature for the user or consumer               | MINOR         |
| `fix`      | A bug fix                                            | PATCH         |
| `docs`     | Documentation only                                   | -             |
| `style`    | Formatting, whitespace, semicolons — no logic change | -             |
| `refactor` | Code change that neither fixes a bug nor adds a feature | -          |
| `perf`     | Performance improvement                              | -             |
| `test`     | Adding or correcting tests                           | -             |
| `build`    | Changes to the build system or dependencies          | -             |
| `ci`       | CI configuration and scripts                         | -             |
| `chore`    | Other changes that don't modify src or test files    | -             |

Only `feat` and `fix` are mandated by the spec. The rest are widely adopted conventions. Pick the type that most accurately describes the change — do not default to `chore` when a more specific type applies.

### Scope (optional)

A noun in parentheses after the type describing the section of the codebase affected:

```
feat(auth): add OAuth2 login flow
fix(parser): handle empty input without crashing
```

Use scope when the repo is large enough that it helps orient the reader. Keep scopes consistent within a project — check `git log` for existing scopes before inventing new ones.

### Description (required)

- Immediately follows the colon and space after the type/scope prefix
- Use the imperative mood ("add", "fix", "remove" — not "added", "fixes", "removed")
- Do not capitalize the first letter
- Do not end with a period
- Keep it under 72 characters
- Summarize *what* the change does, not *how*

### Body (optional)

- Separated from the description by one blank line
- Free-form — can contain multiple paragraphs
- Use to explain *why* the change was made when the description alone is insufficient
- Wrap lines at 72 characters

### Footers (optional)

- Separated from the body (or description if no body) by one blank line
- Format: `token: value` or `token #value`
- Tokens use `-` instead of spaces (e.g., `Reviewed-by`, `Refs`)
- Exception: `BREAKING CHANGE` (must be uppercase, space allowed)

### Breaking changes

Indicate breaking changes in one of two ways (or both):

1. **`!` after type/scope:** `feat(api)!: remove deprecated endpoints`
2. **Footer:** `BREAKING CHANGE: the /v1 endpoints have been removed`

A `BREAKING CHANGE` footer MUST be uppercase. When present, it triggers a MAJOR version bump regardless of the type.

## Choosing the right type

Before writing the message, examine the staged changes:

1. **Does it add new user-facing behavior?** -> `feat`
2. **Does it fix incorrect behavior?** -> `fix`
3. **Does it only change documentation?** -> `docs`
4. **Does it only change tests?** -> `test`
5. **Does it improve performance without changing behavior?** -> `perf`
6. **Does it restructure code without changing behavior?** -> `refactor`
7. **Does it only change formatting/style?** -> `style`
8. **Does it change build tooling or dependencies?** -> `build`
9. **Does it change CI pipelines?** -> `ci`
10. **None of the above?** -> `chore`

If a commit spans multiple types, prefer the most significant one (usually `feat` or `fix`). If the changes are truly unrelated, consider splitting into separate commits.

## Workflow

When asked to commit, or when you determine a commit is needed:

1. Run `git diff --cached` (or `git diff` if nothing is staged) to understand what changed.
2. Run `git log --oneline -10` to see recent commit style and any existing scope conventions.
3. Classify the change using the type selection guide above.
4. Write the commit message following the format rules.
5. Use `write_file` to save the message to a temporary file (e.g., `.git/COMMIT_MESSAGE`) and then use `git commit -F` to commit. This is the most robust way to handle multi-line messages and avoid safety blocks when the message content contains shell-sensitive characters like `$(` or backticks:

```bash
# Step 1: write_file(file_path=".git/COMMIT_MESSAGE", content="...")
# Step 2:
git commit -F .git/COMMIT_MESSAGE && rm .git/COMMIT_MESSAGE
```

## Examples

Simple feature:
```
feat: add email notifications for failed jobs
```

Bug fix with scope:
```
fix(css): correct button alignment on mobile viewports
```

Breaking change with `!` and footer:
```
feat(api)!: require authentication for all endpoints

All API endpoints now require a valid Bearer token.
Previously, read-only endpoints were publicly accessible.

BREAKING CHANGE: unauthenticated requests to any endpoint now return 401
```

Refactor with body:
```
refactor: extract validation logic into shared module

The same validation rules were duplicated across three controllers.
Moving them to a shared module reduces drift and makes the rules
easier to test in isolation.
```

Docs only:
```
docs: add contributing guidelines for new developers
```

Build change:
```
build(deps): upgrade webpack from 4 to 5
```

args.content:
refactor(shell): split aliases and functions to avoid noisy expansions

Restructures .chezmoidata/aliases.yaml to separate aliases (which
remain as abbreviations/expanding aliases) from functions (which
stay as non-expanding shell functions).

This change ensures that common commands like ls, ll, l, and
navigation dots (.., ..., etc.) do not expand into their full
definitions in the terminal, while keeping the useful expansion
behavior for git commands.

- Move ls, ll, l, navigation dots, and cw to a new 'functions' section
- Create dot_config/fish/private_conf.d/functions.fish.tmpl for fish
- Update dot_zshrc.tmpl to define shared functions
- Remove redundant private_cw.fish

refactor(shell): split aliases and functions to avoid noisy expansions

Restructures .chezmoidata/aliases.yaml to separate aliases (which
remain as abbreviations/expanding aliases) from functions (which
stay as non-expanding shell functions).

This change ensures that common commands like ls, ll, l, and
navigation dots (.., ..., etc.) do not expand into their full
definitions in the terminal, while keeping the useful expansion
behavior for git commands.

- Update dot_zshrc.tmpl to define shared functions
